EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and surface trof crossed the region during the afternoon of the 26th. These features...along with diurnal heating...supported the development of thunderstorms across northeast Aroostook county during the afternoon and evening. An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail in Presque Isle.

An intensifying low tracking northeast along the coast approached late on the 5th...crossed the Gulf of Maine and southeast Washington county overnight...then exited across the Maritimes during the 6th. Precipitation expanded across Downeast areas during the morning of the 5th and across the remainder of the region through the afternoon. Snow...heavy at times...then persisted across northern and west-central portions of the region into the evening of the 6th. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred in heavier snow bands from late on the 5th through the early morning hours of the 6th. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the  early morning hours of the 6th. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 15 inches across northern and west-central portions of the region. Wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow along with significantly reduced visibilities and near blizzard conditions at times. Heavy wet snow and winds also brought down trees and power lines. Mixed precipitation and rain limited snow totals across Downeast areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 15 inches. Wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow with near blizzard conditions at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed on the Swift River causing minor flooding for a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed on the Swift River causing minor flooding for a short period of time.  An ice jam briefly formed on the Swift River allowing the river to crest at 8.37' which is 1.37' above the 7' flood stage.  No impacts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north of the region early on the 11th allowing unseasonably warm air to overspread the region. A record high temperature of 55 degrees was set at Bangor on the 11th. Low pressure tracking north of Maine then began to pull the front back south late on the 11th through the morning of the 12th while low pressure tracked along the front. The colder air moving south allowed the rain to transition to heavy snow and sleet across the Saint John Valley and extreme northern Maine through the evening of the 11th. The rain eventually changed to mostly sleet in a zone from northern Piscataquis...to southeast Aroostook...to southern Piscataquis and northern Washington countries.|Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred across the Saint John Valley through the morning of the 12th...with heavy snow and sleet across the remainder of northern portions of Aroostook and Somerset counties. Warning criteria snow and sleet accumulations occurred through the morning of the 12th. Precipitation persisted through the afternoon of the 12th...diminishing late. Storm total snow accumulations across the Saint John Valley ranged from 10 to 14 inches. Across the remainder of northern Aroostook county storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 11 inches along with 1 to 3 inches of sleet. Across the remainder of northern and east-central Maine...storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches along with 1 to 3 inches of sleet. The late arrival of cold air limited wintry precipitation Downeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 1 to 2 inches...along with 1 to 3 inches of sleet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure formed on the morning of the 15th along a stationary boundary and moved up the Ohio River Valley and into the Northeast through the 16th. The system occluded and continued to move northeast, with the triple point passing south of southern New England. Secondary low pressure began to develop at the triple point on the afternoon of the 16th, rapidly deepening as it departed through the Gulf of Maine. The approach of a kicker shortwave helped to maintain an inverted trough northwest of the departing low, concentrating an area of snowfall over parts of eastern New Hampshire and western Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the early morning hours of the 16th. The most persistent and heaviest bands of snow remained near Route 2. Snow was moderate to heavy at times during the day, with an inch or more falling in an hour at times. Snow tapered off in the afternoon before coming to an end. Totals ranged from 4 inches north of Route 16 to 8 inches of heavy, wet snow in the Mahoosuc Mountains .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the Great Lakes on the afternoon of the 18th. Snow moved into the region in the evening and quickly became moderate to heavy at times. Secondary low pressure developed in the vicinity of Long Island and moved into the Gulf of Maine, prolonging snow into the early morning of the 19th. The heaviest snowfall was confined to roughly a 6 hour window centered around 2:00 am on the 19th. The approach of a cold front from the northwest extended snowfall into the mornings hours in the higher terrain before it finally ended as upslope snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on the evening of the 18th and quickly became moderate to heavy through midnight. Snow tapered off but lingered through 6:00 am on the 19th with the approach of a cold front from the west. Behind the front upslope snow showers continued into the afternoon. Snow totals ranged from 5 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure formed on the morning of the 15th along a stationary boundary and moved up the Ohio River Valley and into the Northeast through the 16th. The system occluded and continued to move northeast, with the triple point passing south of southern New England. Secondary low pressure began to develop at the triple point on the afternoon of the 16th, rapidly deepening as it departed through the Gulf of Maine. The approach of a kicker shortwave helped to maintain an inverted trough northwest of the departing low, concentrating an area of snowfall over parts of eastern New Hampshire and western Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the early morning hours of the 16th. The most persistent and heaviest bands of snow remained north of Route 202.  Snow was moderate to heavy at times during the day, with an inch or more falling in an hour at times. Snow tapered off in the afternoon before coming to an end. Totals ranged from 4 inches near the Maine Turnpike to 10 inches of heavy, wet snow north of Route 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure west of the Appalachian Mountains on the 23rd underwent secondary development along a baroclinic zone lingering off the Mid Atlantic coast. That secondary area of low pressure steadily deepened as it moved northeastward into the Gulf of Maine by the 24th. Mid level low centers developed in a location that ensured that warm air struggled to get very far north and prevented most of the mixed precipitation potential. That and a cool and dry antecedent air mass meant that the predominate precipitation type was snow across most of western Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow moved into southwestern Maine during the late afternoon on the 23rd and quickly became moderate to heavy. While forcing was driven primarily by warm air advection, a band of heavy snow developed northwest of the low center and did manage to lift into the area. This extended the duration of snow in addition to producing snowfall rates of one inch per hour at times in the evening. Snow tapered off quickly early in the morning of the 24th. Total snowfall ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

Snowfall rates approached one inch per hour at times in the evening, before tapering off quickly after midnight. Total snowfall ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure developed along an occluded front which crossed the region during the 13th. Warmer air being drawn north allowed precipitation to mix with...or change to...rain across much of northern Maine during the afternoon. However...precipitation was able to remain snow across extreme northern Aroostook county. The snow was heavy at times with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the late afternoon. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches. The greatest snow totals occurred in the Fort Kent...Madawaska and Frenchville areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure drew a cold front across the region through the afternoon into the evening of 13th. A strong low level jet moved along the Downeast coast in advance of the front. Wind gusts along the Downeast coast generally ranged from 40 to 50 mph. However...a 63 mph wind gust was reported near Trescott in coastal Washington county. Trees were also reported down in the Trescott area. Scattered power outages occurred along the entire Downeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 63 mph wind gust was reported near Trescott...along with downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west to northwest winds developed during the 14th with intensifying low pressure across the Maritimes. Wind gusts generally ranged from 35 to 45 mph...with locally stronger gusts in excess of 50 mph. The strong winds combined with recent snow to produce extensive blowing and drifting snow across Aroostook county. The most extensive impacts occurred in open agricultural areas exposed to the winds. Significant blowing and drifting snow...along with reduced visibilities...affected roads in these areas which led to several accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds and recent snow combined to produce extensive blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed across northern Aroostook county during early July and persisted through the remainder of the month. In Caribou...July was the 2nd warmest July on record. July was also the 2nd all-time warmest month in Caribou since records began in 1939. This continued a trend from June. The combined 2-month June-July period was the all-time warmest in Caribou. Dry conditions occurred along with the warm temperatures during June and July. The combined 2-month June-July period was the 3rd driest on record in Caribou. Precipitation was mostly in the form of showers and thunderstorms which produced localized precipitation. The Saint John Valley in particular missed many of the showers and thunderstorms. The 60 to 90 day rain totals across northern Aroostook county were as low as 25 to 50 percent of normal. Near record low stream flows were reported across Aroostook county...particularly early in the month. The dry conditions impacted agricultural interests with reduced yields of hay and grain crops. Increased irrigation was required for potatoes and other crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed in early July and persisted through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold late season upper low supported the development of strong late season low pressure. The intensifying low tracked from the Mid-Atlantic region toward the Gulf of Maine through the early morning hours of the 9th. The intensifying low then tracked from the Gulf of Maine to the Gulf of Saint Lawrence during the 9th while the upper low and cold pool aloft crossed the region. Snow developed across the region through the morning of the 9th. The snow then persisted through the afternoon and evening...heaviest across eastern portions of the region. Across eastern portions of Aroostoook...northern Washington and central Penobscot counties snow bands produced snow rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the afternoon of the 9th. Storm total snow accumulations across these areas generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches...with local totals up to around 15 inches. Lesser snow totals occurred across the remainder of northern and eastern Maine...generally ranging from 2 to 4 inches with 1 to 2 inches along the Downeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 14 inches across southeast areas...tapering to 2 to 5 inches across northwest areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and muggy conditions prevailed over the area as the region had been on the backside of an Atlantic Ridge in the warm sector of an unstable airmass.  A surface cold front was approaching the area from Ontario province along with an upper level trough which was beginning to increase the mid-level shear over the Western Maine Mountains.  A line of thunderstorms developed across the Western Maine Mountains and moved eastward causing isolated damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees uprooted along Stratton Rd in Rangely due to thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure crossed the region...along with an upper low...through the early morning hours of the 12th. Rain transitioned to snow across the Saint John Valley and northern Somerset county during the early morning hours of the 12th then persisted through mid morning. Storm total snow accumulations across the Saint John Valley generally ranged from 2 to 4 inches...with 1 to 2 inches across northern Somerset county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations across the Saint John Valley ranged from 2 to 4 inches.

The intensifying low then tracked from the Gulf of Maine to the Gulf of Saint Lawrence during the 9th while the upper low and cold pool aloft crossed the region. Snow developed across the region through the morning of the 9th. The snow then persisted through the afternoon and evening...heaviest across eastern portions of the region. Across eastern portions of Aroostoook...northern Washington and central Penobscot counties snow bands produced snow rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the afternoon of the 9th. Storm total snow accumulations across these areas generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches...with local totals up to around 15 inches. Lesser snow totals occurred across the remainder of northern and eastern Maine...generally ranging from 2 to 4 inches with 1 to 2 inches along the Downeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 9 to 12 inches...with local totals up to around 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossing the region supported the development of thunderstorms across northern Maine during the afternoon of the 27th. Some of the thunderstorms produced hail.

An unusually cold late season upper low supported the development of strong late season low pressure. The intensifying low tracked from the Mid-Atlantic region toward the Gulf of Maine through the early morning hours of the 9th. The intensifying low then tracked from the Gulf of Maine to the Gulf of Saint Lawrence during the 9th while the upper low and cold pool aloft crossed the region. Snow developed across the region through the morning of the 9th. The snow then persisted through the afternoon and evening...heaviest across eastern portions of the region. Across eastern portions of Aroostoook...northern Washington and central Penobscot counties snow bands produced snow rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the afternoon of the 9th. Storm total snow accumulations across these areas generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches...with local totals up to around 15 inches. Lesser snow totals occurred across the remainder of northern and eastern Maine...generally ranging from 2 to 4 inches with 1 to 2 inches along the Downeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 12 inches across eastern areas...tapering to 3 to 6 inches across western areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure explosively developed in the Gulf of Maine during the afternoon and evening of the 9th...reaching Washington county later that night. The storm produced a nearly record low pressure across portions of the region for the month of April. The low then slowly exited across the Maritimes during the 10th. Snow developed across the region through the afternoon of the 9th. Heavy snow...with rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour at times...occurred through much of the night of the 9th. Lighter snows with the deformation zone then persisted into the 10th before ending late. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred from the late evening of the 9th into the early morning hours of the 10th. Storm total accumulations across northern and central portions of the region generally ranged from 10 to 18 inches...with local totals up to around 20 inches. Snow accumulations across interior Downeast areas generally ranged from 8 to 15 inches. Rain mixed with the snow along the Downeast coast resulting in generally lighter snow accumulations there.|The storm produced a heavy wet snow which stuck to trees and power lines. Across interior locations...the storm produced wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph creating near blizzard conditions at times. The winds and heavy wet snow combined to bring down numerous trees and power lines leading to major power outages across interior areas. A strong low level jet in advance of the low produced wind gusts up to around 50 mph along the Downeast coast contributing to power outages there even with less snow. Power outages rapidly increased through the overnight hours of the 9th into the morning of the 10th. Power outages across northern and eastern Maine peaked at around 80,000 customers during the morning of the 10th...with the most extensive outages across Penobscot county. This was the most extensive loss of power in several years.  Power restoration was not completed until the 15th...even with help from New Hampshire...Vermont and Canada. Another wind event on the 13th led to additional power outages setting back the recovery process.|The storm occurred during a time of high astronomical tides. The high astronomical tides...strong onshore winds and large waves led to coastal flooding around the time of high tide during the early morning hours of the 10th. Several roads along the Downeast coast were overwashed and flooded...with some roads damaged and left debris covered. Some piers were damaged along with shoreline erosion. Several structures around Machias also took on water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Explosively developing low pressure crossing the Gulf of Maine produced strong onshore winds and large waves along the Downeast coast. The storm occurred during a time of high astronomical tides. The strong winds...large waves and high tides led to coastal flooding. Seawall Road was overwashed around the time of high tide. Many rocks...up to around a foot in diameter...and debris covered the road once the waters receded. Plows were required to remove the rocks and debris. This was the most significant overwash event of the season at Seawall Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Aroostook River near Caribou led to flooding along a portion of Grimes Road resulting in closure of the road. The road was closed from the evening of the 5th into the afternoon of the 9th. The waters receded once the jam released allowing the road to re-open.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Aroostook River led to flooding resulting in the closure of Grimes Road. The waters receded once the ice jam released allowing the road to re-open.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of 1.00 to 2.00 inches of rain and snow melt in the headwaters area of the basin led to flooding of the Piscataquis River at Dover-Foxcroft. The river reached flood stage during the early morning hours of the 14th. The river crested around a foot above flood stage later that morning. Flooding impacts were minimal. The river fell below flood stage early that afternoon. Minor flooding was also reported in the Sebec area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Piscataquis River in Dover-Foxcroft from the early morning into the early afternoon. Flooding impacts were minimal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved through southern Canada pushing a warm front and then a cold front through the region, producing 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rain in the Kennebec River basin. This rainfall in combination with melting snow resulted in minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved through southern Canada pushing a warm front and then a cold front through the region, producing 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rain in the Kennebec River basin. This rainfall in combination with melting snow resulted in minor flooding with the North Sidney gauge cresting at 17.2 ft which is .2 ft above the flood stage.  No significant impacts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure approached from the west through the night of the 18th into the morning of the 19th. A secondary low then developed and intensified during the 19th while crossing the Gulf of Maine. The low exited across the Maritimes late on the 19th. Deformation zone snows in the wake of the low persisted through the late evening of the 19th. Snow developed through the evening of the 18th into the early morning hours of the 19th. Snow persisted through the late evening of the 19th. The duration of the generally light snow with this event ranged from around 18 hours to in excess of 24 hours. This duration generally helped limit warning criteria snow accumulations. The exception was across southeast Aroostook county with storm total accumulations of 9 to 12 inches. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the late afternoon of the 19th. Storm total snow accumulations across the remainder of northern Maine generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 9 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of 1.00 to 2.00 inches of rain and snow melt led to minor flooding along the Mattawamkeag River. The river reached flood stage during the late evening of the 14th. The river crested slightly less than a foot above flood stage during the late evening of the 16th. Flooding impacts were minimal...mostly spilling across the marshy flood plain. However...the Bancroft Road between Wytopitlock and Bancroft was occasionally affected along with several camps. The river fell below flood stage during the late evening of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Mattawamkeag River. The flooding mostly affected the marshy flood plain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool and dry air mass was in place across northern New England on May 8th into the 9th. At the same time low pressure began to develop just south of New England spreading rain across Maine on the night of the 8th into the 9th. Through evaporational cooling and strong lift, the rain changed to snow across higher elevations with the snow levels falling throughout the night until snow began to fall even at the coast on the morning of the 9th. Many locations picked up several inches of snow by midday. Accumulating snowfall in early to mid May is exceptionally rare, especially on the coastal plain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across Androscoggin County in the early morning hours on May 9th and accumulated anywhere between a coating and 4 inches by midday. A few selected amounts were 3.5 inches in Turner, 1 inch in Lewiston, and 0.4 inch in Lisbon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure ejected out of the Southwest and reached the East Coast by the 9th. A strong jet streak exiting the Mid Atlantic coast with a coupled jet to the north helped initiate intense secondary cyclogenesis along the New England coast. Pressures in the center of the storm fell over 30 mb in 24 hours by the time it reached peak intensity near Penobscot Bay on the 10th. This storm spread snow over interior areas with rain at the coast. By the afternoon of the 9th rain flipped to heavy, wet snow generally along a north of a line from the White Mountains to Portland, Maine. As snowfall approached and exceeded 4 inches downed limbs began to cause power outages. Where snow exceeded 6 inches the power outages were significant with  100,000 outages reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation overspread the higher terrain of interior Cumberland County during the morning of the 9th as mostly rain at the lower elevations, and some snow in on the peaks. Temperatures gradually cooled into the upper 30s, but by late afternoon heavier precipitation moved in, temperatures fell to the lower 30s, and rain changed to snow. Snow continued moderate to heavy at times into the very early morning hours of the 10th. Snow quickly tapered off early on the morning of the 10th. Snow totals ranged from 5 to 8 inches.

Pressures in the center of the storm fell over 30 mb in 24 hours by the time it reached peak intensity near Penobscot Bay on the 10th. This storm spread snow over interior areas with rain at the coast. By the afternoon of the 9th rain flipped to heavy, wet snow generally along a north of a line from the White Mountains to Portland, Maine. As snowfall approached and exceeded 4 inches downed limbs began to cause power outages. Where snow exceeded 6 inches the power outages were significant with  100,000 outages reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation overspread the Midcoast during the morning of the 9th as mostly rain. Temperatures gradually cooled into the upper 30s, but by late afternoon heavier precipitation moved in, temperatures fell quickly to the lower 30s. Rain quickly changed to heavy snow, and continued moderate to heavy at times through midnight. Snowfall rates exceeded 1 inch per hour at times. Snow quickly tapered off early on the morning of the 10th. Snow totals ranged from 5 inches near the coast to 16 inches inland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool and dry air mass was in place across northern New England on May 8th into the 9th. At the same time low pressure began to develop just south of New England spreading rain across Maine on the night of the 8th into the 9th. Through evaporational cooling and strong lift, the rain changed to snow across higher elevations with the snow levels falling throughout the night until snow began to fall even at the coast on the morning of the 9th. Many locations picked up several inches of snow by midday. Accumulating snowfall in early to mid May is exceptionally rare, especially on the coastal plain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across southern Somerset County in the early morning hours on May 9th and accumulated anywhere between a coating and 4.5 inches by midday. A few selected amounts were 4.2 inches in Hartland, 2.5 inches in Embden, and 1 inch in Harmony.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the coast spread rain into northern New England during the morning hours of May 1. About 1 to 1.5 inches of rain fell during the afternoon of combined with spring snow melt to cause minor flooding in the Swift River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the coast spread rain into northern New England during the morning hours of May 1. An estimated 1 to 1.5 inches fell on already swollen rivers due to late season snow melt from the Western Maine Mountains.  This allowed the river to reach minor flood level of 7.25 ft which is .25 ft above the flood level.  This caused minor flooding on the Swift River in Roxbury with no reported impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure south of Nova Scotia, Canada moved west toward the Maine coast producing 1 to 1.5 inches of rain. This rain combined with melting snow resulted in minor flooding of the Kennebec River at Skowhegan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure south of Nova Scotia, Canada moved west toward the Maine coast producing 1 to 1.5 inches of rain. This rain combined with melting snow resulted in minor flooding of the Kennebec River at Skowhegan.  The Skowhegan gauge crested at 38.4 KCFS which was 3.4 KCFS above flood stage.  No significant impacts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged winter storm affected northern and eastern Maine from the 6th into the early morning hours of the 8th. An initial area of low pressure tracked along the Downeast coast during the 6th. A stronger intensifying low then tracked along the Downeast coast during the 7th. Overrunning snows with the initial low developed during the morning of the 6th. The snow transitioned to a wintry mix across Downeast areas during the afternoon of the 6th...while remaining snow across northern areas. Precipitation remained in the form of snow across northern areas through the night of the 6th...with a wintry mix across central and Downeast portions of the region. Precipitation intensities increased late on the 6th into the 7th with the secondary low. Snow...heavy at times with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour...occurred across northern areas during the 7th. Wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow along with near blizzard conditions at times across northern areas through the afternoon into the evening. Colder air being drawn south allowed precipitation to trend back toward snow across central and Downeast portions of the region through the afternoon and evening of the 7th. The snow then tapered to snow showers through the evening of the 7th into the early morning hours of the 8th. Warning criteria snow and sleet accumulations occurred through the morning and afternoon of the 7th. Where precipitation remained mostly snow across northern Aroostook county...storm total snow accumulations ranged from 15 to 22 inches. The area from northern Somerset to southeast Aroostook county had 8 to 13 inches of snow along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet. The area from southern Piscataquis to northern Washington counties had 5 to 8 inches of snow along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet and up to a quarter inch of ice. Across Downeast areas...snow totals ranged from 2 to 4 inches along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet and a quarter to half an inch of ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 1 to 3 inches...along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet and a quarter to half inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on the 18th a strong high pressure and a fresh cold air mass had settled over New England. Low pressure formed over the Mid Mississippi Valley and tracked through the Great Lakes into southern Canada. Warm air advection around the backside of the high and ahead of the low provided the lift for light snow starting on the morning of the 18th. Light snow continued through the day until a secondary wave of low pressure developed off the southern New England coast in the evening. This produced one final burst of moderate snow before tapering off around midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began on the morning of the 18th ahead of an approaching front. During the afternoon snow became moderate to heavy at times when a secondary wave developed near the southern New England coast. Snow quickly came to an end in the evening, but not before 5 to 7 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across western New England to Quebec province weakened during the 27th...while a secondary low intensified across Maine. The secondary low then lifted across northern Maine through the night of the 7th into the early morning hours of the 8th. Snow expanded across the region through the morning of the 27th. Snow...heavy at times with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour...occurred across northern and east-central areas through the afternoon into the evening of the 27th. The snow diminished during the early morning hours of the 28th. Wind gusts in excess of 30 mph in advance of the secondary low produced extensive blowing and drifting snow...along with near blizzard conditions...across northern and east-central areas during the afternoon and evening of the 27th. Warning criteria snow and sleet accumulations occurred through the afternoon of the 27th. Storm total snow accumulations across northern areas generally ranged from 7 to 11 inches. The area from southern Piscataquis...across central Penobscot..to northern Washington counties generally had 3 to 6 inches of snow along with 1.0 to 1.5 inches of sleet. A wintry mix and rain limited accumulations across Downeast areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of the 6th an upper level warm front lifted into the Northeast. Snow overspread western Maine in the early morning and continued into the mid morning before tapering off. With a dry slot overhead steady precipitation came to an end. Broad warm air advection and onshore flow continued for the rest of the day and through the overnight, producing widespread freezing drizzle and occasional sleet. An upper level trough crossed the Great Lakes on the 7th and interacted with the baroclinic zone off the coast. Low pressure rapidly deepened along the front and lifted northeast into the Gulf of Maine. While damaging wind gusts remained south of the area, a band of heavy snow developed in the western Maine mountains. The band moved east during the evening of the 7th and ended as upslope snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the mid level warm front moved through the area on the morning of the 6th as steady light snow overspread northern Oxford County. A fairly uniform 3 to 4 inches of snow fell before it tapered off by midday. A prolonged period of no precipitation or light freezing drizzle or sleet lasted until the morning off the 7th. It was during the morning hours that a nearly stationary band of moderate to heavy snow develop overhead in response to rapidly strengthening low pressure in the Gulf of Maine. As the band finally moved east in the late afternoon precipitation ended as a period of upslope snow showers. Snow totals ranged from 6 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of the 26th a weak low pressure formed over the Piedmont of North Carolina and tracked northeastward along the Mid Atlantic coast. Low pressure deepened more rapidly as it moved through New England just inland from the coast. Through much of the day on the 26th there was only showery precipitation near the warm front as it moved northward. A larger and heavier area of precipitation moved into the region just ahead of the main surface low. Widespread heavy rainfall resulted south of the mountains, but in the higher terrain and to the north a heavy, wet snow fell. Steady precipitation was quick to come to an end on the afternoon of the 27th, but upslope snow showers continued into the overnight as much colder air poured into the Northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mid level warm front lifting northward brought snow showers to the area for much of the day on the 26th. A break in precipitation occurred overnight followed by a large area of heavy precipitation moving in before dawn on the 27th. Heavy, wet snow came down for most of the first half of the day before tapering off to a drier and lighter snowfall in the afternoon. Steady snow came to an end in the late afternoon but upslope snow showers continued into the overnight. Total snowfall ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

An initial area of low pressure tracked along the Downeast coast during the 6th. A stronger intensifying low then tracked along the Downeast coast during the 7th. Overrunning snows with the initial low developed during the morning of the 6th. The snow transitioned to a wintry mix across Downeast areas during the afternoon of the 6th...while remaining snow across northern areas. Precipitation remained in the form of snow across northern areas through the night of the 6th...with a wintry mix across central and Downeast portions of the region. Precipitation intensities increased late on the 6th into the 7th with the secondary low. Snow...heavy at times with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour...occurred across northern areas during the 7th. Wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow along with near blizzard conditions at times across northern areas through the afternoon into the evening. Colder air being drawn south allowed precipitation to trend back toward snow across central and Downeast portions of the region through the afternoon and evening of the 7th. The snow then tapered to snow showers through the evening of the 7th into the early morning hours of the 8th. Warning criteria snow and sleet accumulations occurred through the morning and afternoon of the 7th. Where precipitation remained mostly snow across northern Aroostook county...storm total snow accumulations ranged from 15 to 22 inches. The area from northern Somerset to southeast Aroostook county had 8 to 13 inches of snow along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet. The area from southern Piscataquis to northern Washington counties had 5 to 8 inches of snow along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet and up to a quarter inch of ice. Across Downeast areas...snow totals ranged from 2 to 4 inches along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet and a quarter to half an inch of ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 4 inches...along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet and a quarter to half inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossing northern Maine...along with diurnal heating...supported the development of thunderstorms across Aroostook county during the afternoon and evening of the 9th. Isolated severe thunderstorms produced localized damaging winds and large hail across northeast Aroostook county.

Above normal level temperatures occurred during November...with some areas experiencing several days of record warmth. All-time monthly temperature records for November occurred at several locations. Variable rainfall occurred during the month...with stretches of both wet and dry conditions. The variability led to some areas receiving above normal monthly rainfall...with other areas below normal. Severe drought ( D2 ) persisted through the month across Hancock county and coastal portions of Washington county. However...a significant rain event at the end of the month supported the potential for improving conditions going into December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought ( D2 ) persisted during November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak warm front pushed north of the region allowing a hot and unstable airmass to move into southeast Maine.  A weak mid-level shortwave moved across the state during the day which increased the shear and mid-level lapse rates.  Storms developed by noon across the foothills of Maine and progressed eastward through the afternoon with multiple storms becoming severe.  Large hail and damaging wind gusts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported near Fayette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was under a broad warm sector with a moist and unstable airmass firmly entrenched.  An upper level trough sharpened across the region as a strong cold front swung through the state during the afternoon. Numerous storms developed along the Western Mountains and continued to strengthen over the foothills were they became severe.  Many storms maintained strength as the traveled eastward all the way to the coast.  Numerous reports of wind damage were reported across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires reported down in Norway and Waterford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias was the first tropical storm to impact Maine since 2011.  The center of the storm tracked well west of the state, keeping all the heavy rain associated with the storm across New York.  The primary impacts the storm brought to southwest Maine were gusty winds with widespread reports of wind gusts in the mid to upper 40s.  Numerous trees and branches were brought down with the winds resulting in peak power outages around 125,000 customers.  Additional boat damage was also reported on some area lakes due to the strong winds.  Overall storm impacts were brief with a period of gusty winds from the south to southeast on the evening of August 4th causing most of the damage. No coastal flooding was reported with the storm as the minor storm surge with this event of around 1 foot coincided at low tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias brought moderate impacts to Cumberland County due wind gusts up to 45 mph. Numerous snapped and uprooted trees along with broken branches caused downed power lines through the county. Cumberland County was one of the harder hit areas for power outages with 28,000 customers losing power, with a restoration time for most outages within 24 hours. A number of boats were grounded or sunk on Sebago lake, with a unverified home weather station reported wind gust on the lakefront of 66 mph.  Hundreds of trees were uprooted on the northern end of the lake. No flooding was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought persisted across northern Aroostok county during August. The severe drought across northern Aroostook county had developed in early July. Above average temperatures were reported during August. Rainfall was mostly due to showers and thunderstorms...which resulted in variable rain totals. Most areas received 40 to 70 percent of normal rainfall for August...though some localized areas did receive greater rain totals with thunderstorms. Widespread rain did not occur during August. In Caribou...the 3 month period from June to August was the warmest and second driest on record. |Streamflows during August were generally at 15 to 35 percent of normal. At month's end many larger rivers were at near record low levels. Grain crops were significantly impacted during August. Potatoes and other crops required extensive irrigation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought conditions which had developed in early July persisted through the month of August.

The primary impacts the storm brought to southwest Maine were gusty winds with widespread reports of wind gusts in the mid to upper 40s.  Numerous trees and branches were brought down with the winds resulting in peak power outages around 125,000 customers.  Additional boat damage was also reported on some area lakes due to the strong winds.  Overall storm impacts were brief with a period of gusty winds from the south to southeast on the evening of August 4th causing most of the damage. No coastal flooding was reported with the storm as the minor storm surge with this event of around 1 foot coincided at low tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias brought limited impacts to Franklin County due wind gusts up to 45 mph. Scattered reports of snapped and uprooted trees along with broken branches caused downed power lines through the county. Franklin County reported 3,000 customers without power, with a restoration time for most outages within 24 hours. No flooding was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak warm front pushed north of the region allowing a hot and unstable airmass to move into southeast Maine.  A weak mid-level shortwave moved across the state during the day which increased the shear and mid-level lapse rates.  Storms developed by noon across the foothills of Maine and progressed eastward through the afternoon with multiple storms becoming severe.  Large hail and damaging wind gusts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to ping pong ball size near Livermore Falls. Some crop damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was under a broad warm sector with a moist and unstable airmass firmly entrenched.  An upper level trough sharpened across the region as a strong cold front swung through the state during the afternoon. Numerous storms developed along the Western Mountains and continued to strengthen over the foothills were they became severe.  Many storms maintained strength as the traveled eastward all the way to the coast.  Numerous reports of wind damage were reported across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two downed trees just northeast of Fayette. One large pine crashed into a house cause structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought expanded across portions of central and southern Penobscot county...along with Hancock county...during September. Rain totals during the first 10 days of the month were generally less than 0.10 inch Severe drought expanded across these areas during the third week of September and persisted through the remainder of the month. Bangor experienced its driest September on record with only 0.28 inches of rain. This also represented the all time third driest month on record. Record...or near record...low river and stream levels were reported at month's end. Crops were impacted to varying degrees by the drought conditions. Grain crop yields were reduced, with hay at about half of normal. Potatoes...blueberries...apples and other crops were also affected. The impact on the potato crop was dependent on the extent of irrigation. Harvesting of many crops was in progress at months end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought expanded across the region during the third week of the month and persisted through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across Downeast areas during the afternoon of the 25th in advance of a strong cold front crossing the region. Some of the thunderstorms turned severe...producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous power outages were reported in the Orono area due to downed power lines from wind gusts estimated at up to 65 mph. Hail up to 0.70 inch in diameter was also reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought expanded across southeast Aroostook and southern Piscataquis counties during mid August and persisted through the remainder of the month. These areas were in moderate drought at the start of the month. Above average temperatures were reported during August. Rainfall was mostly due to showers and thunderstorms which resulted in variable rain totals. Most areas received 40 to 70 percent of normal rainfall for August...though some areas did receive greater totals with thunderstorms. Widespread rain did not occur during August. In Houlton...the three month period from June to August was the 5th warmest and all-time driest on record. Streamflows during August were generally 15 to 35 percent of normal. At month's end many of the larger rivers were at near record low levels. Grain crops were significantly impacted during August. Potatoes and other crops required extensive irrigation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed during mid August and persisted through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed across central Somerset County by early August and persisted through the remainder of the month. Dry conditions developed rapidly across western Maine starting in the middle of May. The period May 16 to June 25 was exceedingly dry. There was some relief in late June and July with showers and thunderstorms, but Somerset County mostly missed out on any widespread rainfall.  The 60 to 90 day rainfall totals across this region were generally only 25 to 35 percent of normal. River flows have been low since the middle of June when they were at their lowest. Since then summertime showers and thunderstorms have caused an up and down nature to river levels with temporary but brief relief. As of August 31st, 31 percent of the river gages maintained by the USGS in Maine are indicating below normal stream flow another 28 percent are much below normal and 8 percent are at near record low flow for the time of year.  Agriculture impacts were primarily focused on the hay crop with well below normal yield on the first cut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed in North Central Somerset County during the month of August as short term drought conditions continued to worsen and intensify across the county in August.  This region experienced one of the driest late spring through summer periods on record with rainfall deficits estimated to be 5 to 6 below normal.  ||River flows have been low since the middle of June when they were at their lowest. Since then summertime showers and thunderstorms have caused an up and down nature to river levels with temporary relief. Rivers are steadily falling now and with little precipitation expected over the next few days this will continue. As of August 31st, 31 percent of the river gages maintained by the USGS in Maine are indicating below normal stream flow another 28 percent are much below normal and 8 percent are at near record low flow for the time of year.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unusually dry conditions which initiatlly developed from late May to early June persisted during September. Thus...areas in severe drought at the end of August generally remained in severe drought through September. In addition...a small area of extreme drought developed across portions of northeast Aroostook and northern Penobscot counties mid month and persisted through the remainder of the month. Rain totals during the first 10 days of the month were generally less than 0.10 inch. Monthly September rainfall ranged from 5 to 40 percent of normal. Houlton experienced its driest September on record with 0.50 inches. Caribou experienced the second driest September on record with 1.15 inches. However...1.01 inches of that total fell on the 30th. The period from June through September was the driest on record for both Caribou and Houlton. On September 21st Aroostook county was declared a Drought Disaster Area. Rain from Hurricane Teddy brought some drought relief to portions of Eastern Washington county...with some locations receiving in excess of 1.50 inches of rain. Many rivers and streams remained at record...or near record...low levels during September. Some dug wells also ran dry. The drought affected most crops to varying extents. Grain crop harvests were reduced, with hay at around half of normal levels. Impacts were also reported with potatoes...blueberries...apples and other items. The impact on the potato crop varied dependent on the extent of irrigation. Harvesting operations for many crops were in progress at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought persisted through the month. An area of extreme drought also developed mid month and persisted through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought expanded across much of the remainder of northern Maine during mid August. The severe drought also expanded across the remainder of Piscataquis county and all of Washington county. Severe drought then persisted in all of these areas through the remainder of the month. These areas had been in moderate drought at the start of August. Above average temperatures occurred during August. Rainfall was mostly due to showers and thunderstorms which resulted in variable rain totals. Most areas received 40 to 70 percent of normal rainfall for August...though some localized areas did receive greater rain totals with thunderstorms. Widespread rain did not occur during August. The accumulated effect of warm and dry conditions for the 4 month period from May to August led to the expansion of the severe drought. Streamflows during August were generally 15 to 35 percent of normal. At month's end many larger rivers were at near record low levels. Across southern Washington county...some wells were starting to run dry at month's end. Grain crops were significantly impacted during August. Potatoes and other crops required extensive irrigation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed mid August then persisted through the remainder of the month.

Rain from Hurricane Teddy brought some drought relief to portions of Eastern Washington county...with some locations receiving in excess of 1.50 inches of rain. Many rivers and streams remained at record...or near record...low levels during September. Some dug wells also ran dry. The drought affected most crops to varying extents. Grain crop harvests were reduced, with hay at around half of normal levels. Impacts were also reported with potatoes...blueberries...apples and other items. The impact on the potato crop varied dependent on the extent of irrigation. Harvesting operations for many crops were in progress at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought persisted through the month. An area of extreme drought also developed mid month and persisted through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought expanded across portions of central and southern Penobscot county...along with Hancock county...during September. Rain totals during the first 10 days of the month were generally less than 0.10 inch Severe drought expanded across these areas during the third week of September and persisted through the remainder of the month. Bangor experienced its driest September on record with only 0.28 inches of rain. This also represented the all time third driest month on record. Record...or near record...low river and stream levels were reported at month's end. Crops were impacted to varying degrees by the drought conditions. Grain crop yields were reduced, with hay at about half of normal. Potatoes...blueberries...apples and other crops were also affected. The impact on the potato crop was dependent on the extent of irrigation. Harvesting of many crops was in progress at months end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought expanded across the region during the third week of the month and persisted through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought expanded across much of the remainder of northern Maine during mid August. The severe drought also expanded across the remainder of Piscataquis county and all of Washington county. Severe drought then persisted in all of these areas through the remainder of the month. These areas had been in moderate drought at the start of August. Above average temperatures occurred during August. Rainfall was mostly due to showers and thunderstorms which resulted in variable rain totals. Most areas received 40 to 70 percent of normal rainfall for August...though some localized areas did receive greater rain totals with thunderstorms. Widespread rain did not occur during August. The accumulated effect of warm and dry conditions for the 4 month period from May to August led to the expansion of the severe drought. Streamflows during August were generally 15 to 35 percent of normal. At month's end many larger rivers were at near record low levels. Across southern Washington county...some wells were starting to run dry at month's end. Grain crops were significantly impacted during August. Potatoes and other crops required extensive irrigation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed mid August then persisted through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked north across New England and Quebec province...to the west of the region...from the 29th into the 30th. The low drew a cold front across the region during the 30th. A strong southerly low level jet crossed the region in advance of the front. Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of 45 to around 60 mph...were common in advance of the front. A 62 mph wind gust was measured at Bar Harbor. In addition...a narrow line of heavy convective showers developed along the front which helped mix stronger winds aloft to the surface. The greater impacts of the squall line occurred across portions of eastern Aroostook county where wind gusts were locally estimated up to around 70 mph.|The winds toppled or snapped numerous trees and broke branches...which in many instances brought down power lines. Power outages across northern and eastern Maine peaked at nearly 30,000 customers during the afternoon of the 30th. Approximately half of these outages were around the greater Bangor area in southern Penobscot county.  Some trees were toppled on to homes resulting in varying degrees of structural damage. The winds also ripped shingles and siding from some structures along with damaging roofs. The aluminum roof was torn from a residential building in Mapleton...northeast Aroostook county. The roof was also partially torn from an aircraft hanger at the former Loring Air Force Base...also in northeast Aroostook county...from estimated 70 mph wind gusts associated with the squall line. Fallen trees and downed wires led to the closure of some roads across the region. Wind impacts were particularly concentrated along the Downeast coast...southern Penobscot county and portions of eastern Aroostook county.  Trees still mostly leaved...along with wet ground...helped enhance the wind damage. Across the remainder of northern and eastern Maine sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts up to around 50 mph...also brought down trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with estimated gusts up to 60 mph...brought down trees and power lines. A wind gust of 52 mph occurred at the Bangor International Airport. Power outages in the greater Bangor area peaked at around 14,000 customers during the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 16th around 09:30 a landslide occurred in Westbrook near the Sappi Mill.  The landslide immediately blocked the entire channel of the Presumscot River.  River levels immediately began to rise as the landslide dammed the river.  Due to the ongoing drought and low river flow, it took hours for the backwater to reach action stage.  Fortunately, once the river approached flood stage, a small channel opened in the newly formed dam allowing water to flow through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On September 16th around 09:30 a landslide occurred in Westbrook near the Sappi Mill. The landslide immediately blocked the entire channel of the Presumscot River. River levels immediately began to rise as the landslide dammed the river. Due to the ongoing drought and low river flow, it took hours for the backwater to reach action stage. Fortunately, once the river approached flood stage, a small channel opened in the newly formed dam allowing water to flow through it.  The river remained elevated near flood stage for numerous days until the increased flow an erosion of the landside caused the river level to drop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Teddy underwent extra-tropical transition to a strong 951 mb storm well off shore in the Gulf of Maine before moving north north towards Halifax, Nova Scotia. This powerful cyclone coincided with astronomically high tides.  Although winds were offshore, large, long period swell entered the Gulf of Maine leading to splash-over along the southern coast of Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The astronomical tide in Portland of 10.8 feet combined with a storm surge of 1.3 feet at high tide to reach a 12.1 foot storm tide during the mid afternoon hours. Flood stage in Portland is 12.0 feet (MLLW).  The tide remained above flood stage for 30 minutes at the Portland tide gage.  The waves took down a fence at Higgin���s Beach in Scarborough with minor flooding reported along the wharf streets in Portland.

Significant splash-over also occurred along Gooch���s and Kennebunk Beaches in Kennebunk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions developed rapidly across western Maine starting in the middle of May. The period May 16 to June 25 was exceedingly dry. There was some relief in late June and July, especially in the Western Maine Mountains and parts of the Mid-Coast.  Southern Maine. By the middle of August dry conditions set in again with September being exceedingly dry with some locations reporting their driest September on record. The National Centers for Environmental Information reported that September was the driest on record for Maine.  D2 severe drought was confined to parts of York and Somerset County at the beginning of the September.  The D2 drought rapidly expanded into Oxford, Cumberland, Androscoggin and Franklin Counties by the middle of September.  By the end of September D2 drought expanded across all of Western Maine, with a D3 extreme drought area developing across York and parts of Cumberland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought continued to expand and intensified through the month of September with the state of Maine recording the driest September on record.  D2 severe drought expanded to cover all of Western Maine by the end of the month.  In addition, an area of D3 extreme drought developed across York County and southwest Cumberland County by the end of the month.   Since Mid-May Portland was 8.5 below normal while Augusta was 5.75 below normal.  Hydrologic conditions continued to worsen in September on non-regulated rivers with 90% of USGS river gaging stations below normal and a few sites at record lows for the end of September.  Reservoir levels on the Androscoggin and Kennebec Rivers remained near normal due to decreased flow and proper water management on these systems.  Numerous reports of natural lake levels were well below normal though. In Maine of the 10 monitoring wells maintained by the USGS in western Maine 5 are below normal, 1 is much below normal, 4 are in the normal range.  In Maine, all 16 counties reported dry wells with  190 being reported to state agencies.  The USDA Farm Service Agency reported every county in Maine, except Knox, has received a drought declaration.  Crops across Maine have been hurt by the dry weather. Corn, potatoes, blueberries, barley and forage crops are the most affected. Farmers are concerned about having enough forage for their livestock to get through the winter.  State level agriculture assistance programs, not counting insurance claims, have paid out over $500,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked north across New England and Quebec province...to the west of the region...from the 29th into the 30th. The low drew a cold front across the region during the 30th. A strong southerly low level jet crossed the region in advance of the front. Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of 45 to around 60 mph...were common in advance of the front. A 62 mph wind gust was measured at Bar Harbor. In addition...a narrow line of heavy convective showers developed along the front which helped mix stronger winds aloft to the surface. The greater impacts of the squall line occurred across portions of eastern Aroostook county where wind gusts were locally estimated up to around 70 mph.|The winds toppled or snapped numerous trees and broke branches...which in many instances brought down power lines. Power outages across northern and eastern Maine peaked at nearly 30,000 customers during the afternoon of the 30th. Approximately half of these outages were around the greater Bangor area in southern Penobscot county.  Some trees were toppled on to homes resulting in varying degrees of structural damage. The winds also ripped shingles and siding from some structures along with damaging roofs. The aluminum roof was torn from a residential building in Mapleton...northeast Aroostook county. The roof was also partially torn from an aircraft hanger at the former Loring Air Force Base...also in northeast Aroostook county...from estimated 70 mph wind gusts associated with the squall line. Fallen trees and downed wires led to the closure of some roads across the region. Wind impacts were particularly concentrated along the Downeast coast...southern Penobscot county and portions of eastern Aroostook county.  Trees still mostly leaved...along with wet ground...helped enhance the wind damage. Across the remainder of northern and eastern Maine sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts up to around 50 mph...also brought down trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts up to around 60 mph...brought down trees and power lines. A wind gust of 62 mph occurred at the Bar Harbor Airport.

The National Centers for Environmental Information reported that September was the driest on record for Maine.  D2 severe drought was confined to parts of York and Somerset County at the beginning of the September.  The D2 drought rapidly expanded into Oxford, Cumberland, Androscoggin and Franklin Counties by the middle of September.  By the end of September D2 drought expanded across all of Western Maine, with a D3 extreme drought area developing across York and parts of Cumberland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought continued to expand and intensified through the month of September with the state of Maine recording the driest September on record.  D2 severe drought expanded to cover all of Western Maine by the end of the month.  In addition, an area of D3 extreme drought developed across York County and southwest Cumberland County by the end of the month.   Since Mid-May Portland was 8.5 below normal while Augusta was 5.75 below normal.  Hydrologic conditions continued to worsen in September on non-regulated rivers with 90% of USGS river gaging stations below normal and a few sites at record lows for the end of September.  Reservoir levels on the Androscoggin and Kennebec Rivers remained near normal due to decreased flow and proper water management on these systems.  Numerous reports of natural lake levels were well below normal though..  In Maine of the 10 monitoring wells maintained by the USGS in western Maine 5 are below normal, 1 is much below normal, 4 are in the normal range.  In Maine, all 16 counties reported dry wells with  190 being reported to state agencies.  The USDA Farm Service Agency reported every county in Maine, except Knox, has received a drought declaration.  Crops across Maine have been hurt by the dry weather. Corn, potatoes, blueberries, barley and forage crops are the most affected. Farmers are concerned about having enough forage for their livestock to get through the winter.  State level agriculture assistance programs, not counting insurance claims, have paid out over $500,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked to the west of the region on the evening of Nov 30th with a broad area of moderate to heavy rain and pockets of embedded convection enveloping the region.  Widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 occurred with localized amounts approaching 3 in the favorable mountain upslope areas.  Partially frozen ground and dormant vegetation status led to rapid run-off into area rivers.

Rainfall reports of 2.5 were reported in the Kennebec River Basin, which led to rapid river rises.  The Kennebec River at Augusta crested in minor flood stage at 14.1 ft, flood stage is 14 ft. No significant impacts were reported with minor low land flooding.

Melting snow and partially frozen ground led to excessive runoff and resulted in minor river flooding across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous warm and humid airmass interacted with a strong cold surface cold front that allowed for late season thunderstorms to develop. In addition, very steep mid-level lapse rates along with strong shear allowed a few of these thunderstorms to become severe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modest low pressure system developed along the Gulf Coast on the 16th, with secondary development occurring off the Carolinas by the afternoon. An Arctic air mass was in place over New England as a strong high pressure anchored itself in the Saint Lawrence River Valley. Low pressure occluded early on the 17th and slowly tracked south of New England through the day. An intense band of heavy snow moved north into parts of western Maine early in the morning on the 17th and remained nearly stationary into the afternoon. Snowfall rates of 4 to 6 inches per hour and total amounts of 1 to 2 feet were observed at the peak of the event. Dry air and a deamplifying upper trough limited the northward push of heavy snow, leaving a sharp gradient on the poleward edge of accumulating snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began before sunrise on the 17th. Snow quickly became heavy as an intense band of snow moved into the area. As the band became nearly stationary snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour were observed for several hours. A weakening trough aloft quickly brought an end to snow by mid afternoon. Snowfall totals ranged between 14 and 20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked northwest of Maine from the 15th into the 16th...while a secondary low developed and tracked across the region. A maximum wind gust of 68 mph was reported in Trescott. A wind gust of 61 mph was reported in Eastport.  The winds brought down trees and power lines leading to scattered power outages. Damage to some roofs was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossing northern Maine...along with diurnal heating...supported the development of thunderstorms across Aroostook county during the afternoon and evening of the 9th. Isolated severe thunderstorms produced localized damaging winds and large hail across northeast Aroostook county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were toppled on State Road by wind gusts estimated at up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked to the west of the area across Western New York on the night of November 30th before stalling by the morning of December first.  A warm and moist Atlantic moisture fetch allowed for an anomalous southeasterly airmass for the beginning of December to move into the region.  Heavy rain occurred across the region with widespread reports of 2 to 3 of rain being reported across the eastern slopes of the Western Maine Mountains.  This led to localized flooding and minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain with measured amounts of 2.82 being reported in the vicinity of Newry, ME lead to localized flash flooding at the Sunday River Ski Resort.  In addition to the heavy rain, melting man made snow contributed to rapid runoff.  Heavy erosion and undermining caused damage to one of the base areas at Sunday River Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and muggy conditions prevailed over the area as the region had been on the backside of an Atlantic Ridge in the warm sector of an unstable air mass.  A strengthening area of low pressure began to approach western Maine by midday along with a surface cold front.  A compact shortwave upper level trough dug into the region from Ontario increased the mid-level shear over Maine. Two distinct rounds of thunderstorms crossed the state with the first pre-frontal trough bringing damaging wind gusts around noon, with a second round of damaging thunderstorms by mid-afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three quarter inch hail and gusty winds was reported by a weather spotter on the Hiram and Baldwin by a weather spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A change in the pattern led to more frequent rain events across much of northern and eastern Maine during October. The change in pattern developed early in the month and persisted through the month. The first rain event occurred on the 2nd...with more frequent rain events occurring through the month. The two largest rain events occurred on the 13th/14th and 16th/17th. During each of these events much of northern Maine and the central highlands received 1 to 3 inch rain totals...with locally greater amounts. The monthly rain total of 7.21 inches at Caribou was the 3rd wettest October on record. The 6.48 inches at Millinocket was the 15th wettest...the 6.08 inches at Houlton was the 9th wettest. Monthly rain totals were generally 130 - 220% of normal across central and northern portions of the region...with the greater departures above normal across northern areas and the central highlands. Rain totals across Downeast areas were more variable...ranging from slightly above normal to below normal.|Rivers and streams responded rapidly to the increased rainfall. Many larger rivers were at record low levels at the start of the month. At months end...many of the rivers across northern areas and the central highlands had recovered to 100 - 250% of normal levels for late October. With the growing season ending and most harvesting finished by months end...agricultural impacts during October were minimized. Much of northern and eastern Maine was in severe drought ( D2 )...with pockets of extreme drought ( D3 )...at the start of the month. At months end...most of northern and eastern Maine had improved to moderate drought ( D1 ) or just abnormally dry ( D0 ).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved through the month. At months end...conditions ranged from moderate drought ( D1 ) to just abnormally dry ( D0 ).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and muggy conditions prevailed over the area as the region had been on the backside of an Atlantic Ridge in the warm sector of an unstable air mass.  A strengthening area of low pressure began to approach western Maine by midday along with a surface cold front.  A compact shortwave upper level trough dug into the region from Ontario increased the mid-level shear over Maine. Two distinct rounds of thunderstorms crossed the state with the first pre-frontal trough bringing damaging wind gusts around noon, with a second round of damaging thunderstorms by mid-afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm wind gusts downed trees and wires on Quarry Road in New Gloucester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions developed rapidly across western Maine starting in the middle of May. The period May 16 to June 25 was exceedingly dry. There was some relief in late June and July, especially in the Western Maine Mountains and parts of the Mid-Coast. Southern Maine. By the middle of August dry conditions set in again with September being exceedingly dry with some locations reporting their driest September on record. The National Centers for Environmental Information reported that September was the driest on record for Maine. D2 severe drought was confined to parts of York and Somerset County at the beginning of the September. The D2 drought rapidly expanded into Oxford, Cumberland, Androscoggin and Franklin Counties by the middle of September. By the end of September D2 drought expanded across all of Western Maine, with a D3 extreme drought area developing across York and parts of Cumberland Counties.  Through the first 2 weeks of October the drought classification remained similar across the region with no significant rainfall.  Things changed on October 13th with a significant rain storm bringing welcome relieve to the drought as a widespread 2 to 3 of rainfall occurred.  More significant rain occurred on the 16-17th with 1 to 2 of rainfall across the region.  By the 3rd week of October the drought classification began to improve with Severe Drought being reduced to Moderate Drought across the Western Mountains and Foothills.  D2 drought classification remained along the coastal counties and parts of Androscoggin and Kennebec Counties.  Southern York County remained in D3 drought through the end of the month with significant rainfall departures remaining.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought maintained through the beginning of October with no significant rainfall across the region.  Things changed on October 13th when a significant rainstorm brought widespread rainfall across the region.  Another significant rainstorm occurred on October 16-17th with more beneficial rainfall.  Portland reported an October monthly total rainfall of 4.08, Augusta reported 5.69 and Rangeley reported 6.49. The above normal rainfall across the Western Mountains and foothills allowed for the D2 Severe drought classification to be lowered to D1 Moderate Drought by the third week of October. D2 drought classification remained along the coastal counties and parts of Androscoggin and Kennebec Counties. Southern York County remained in D3 drought through the end of the month with significant rainfall departures remaining. Hydrologic conditions continued to worsen during the first week 2 weeks of October on non-regulated rivers with 90% of USGS river gaging stations below normal and a few sites at record lows for the beginning of October as river levels are normally beginning to rebound from the seasonal low flows. After the mid-month rains, hydrologic conditions significantly improved with many rivers systems in the Western Maine mountains returning to normal levels with flow remaining below normal on some smaller coastal basins. Reservoir levels on the Androscoggin and Kennebec Rivers remained near normal due to decreased flow and proper water management on these systems. Numerous reports of natural lake levels remained below normal for the month. In Western Maine, the 10 monitoring wells maintained by the USGS, 4 were below normal, 1 was much below normal, and 5 were in the normal range.  In Maine, all 16 counties reported dry wells with  364 being reported to state agencies. The USDA Farm Service Agency reported every county in Maine, had received a drought declaration. Crops across Maine were hurt by the dry weather. Corn, potatoes, blueberries, barley and forage crops are the most affected. Farmers are concerned about having enough forage for their livestock to get through the winter. State level agriculture assistance programs, not counting insurance claims, have paid out over $500,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked across Maine during the 17th...along with an approaching upper low. Cold air wrapping around the surface low and the cold pool aloft with the upper low allowed rain to transition to snow across northern Somerset county during the morning. A heavy wet snow then persisted through much of the afternoon before ending late. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the afternoon. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches. The heavy wet snow did bring down some small trees and contributed to some power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system approaching from Eastern Ontario brought cool, wet conditions to the region. Upper air observations in the region indicated that temperatures at the top of Mt. Katahdin started just above freezing, then dropped just below freezing after the front passed. Winds during this time increased significantly as well, reaching close to 50 knots at mountain top by the morning of October 8th. These conditions, although not unseasonable, would have been dangerously cold for unprepared hikers, and resulted in the deaths of 2 individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 75 year old hiker and his nephew lost their way on Mt Katahdin. While they were waiting for rescuers, the older man fell 50 feet and became injured. During the night he became hypothermic due to the cold, wet conditions. He passed away the next day from his injuries.
